data.title: Artisan Select Equity Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
AI Everything

Ho-hum, technology stocks did better than everything else this quarter. We’ve delivered this same message frequently over the past five years. But the numbers are now mind-boggling. For example, here is an eye-opener: $3 trillion. That’s how much market cap the five top contributing stocks in the S&P 500® Index (all of them tech companies) added in three months. That’s equal to 10 Coca-Cola companies. Or 6 Exxon Mobils. Or 3.5 JP Morgan Chases. It’s about the same size as the UK stock market. The US central bank’s gold reserves are valued at “only” about a trillion dollars.

This is what it takes to make gold:

Gold forms during cataclysmic cosmic events like supernovae and neutron star collisions over billions of years, long before Earth existed. These events provide the intense energy needed for the r-process, which creates heavy elements like gold. The gold travels through space as cosmic dust, eventually falling onto and forming planets such as Earth. Over time, the gold on Earth became concentrated in accessible deposits through geological processes.

Billions of years? Travelling through space? The stock market mints multiples of our nation’s gold reserves in a few months.

We are witnesses to an almost supernatural money-minting machine. That machine is artificial intelligence (AI), or more specifically, OpenAI, the owner of ChatGPT. OpenAI is a private company with a roughly $13 billion annual revenue run rate and $18 billion in cash on its balance sheet, and it’s burning $8 billion–$9 billion per year. Its October 2025 valuation pegs OpenAI’s market value at $500 billion. (Note that the most valuable publicly traded company in all of Europe is worth about $375 billion.) OpenAI’s valuation is not even the interesting part. By our estimates, this one money-losing company has committed to be the anchor customer for $500 billion or more in AI data center investment over the next four years.

In January, President Donald Trump and representatives from OpenAI, Oracle (ORCL) and Softbank (OTCPK:SFTBY) announced the Stargate project, the largest AI infrastructure project in history. The venture launched with an initial investment of $100 billion, with plans to increase to $500 billion by 2029. It is unclear whether OpenAI will invest directly in the project, though it appears that Stargate’s infrastructure will be used exclusively by OpenAI. How a company burning $8 billion–$9 billion per year is going to be the anchor customer for a $500 billion investment is a good question. Nevertheless, since the announcement, Softbank’s market cap has increased by nearly $100 billion and Oracle’s by $350 billion. We can imagine AI executives asking each other, “How many Coca-Cola Companies did YOU add this year?” In the case of Softbank, not even half. But in the case of Oracle, 1.2X (and we still have a quarter to go before year-end.)

In September, Oracle announced a “mega” contract from OpenAI. We don’t know how much OpenAI can afford to spend with Oracle, but we do know that Oracle’s market cap on the day of the announcement jumped by $250 billion, about one Coca-Cola Company.

In September, NVIDIA (NVDA) announced a partnership with OpenAI to deploy at least 10 gigawatts (GW) of AI data centers powered by NVIDIA systems. NVIDIA will invest up to $100 billion in OpenAI as each GW is deployed. The structure of this deal is interesting. Clearly, investing hundreds of billions of dollars in projects that rely on one customer, which arguably does not have the resources to commit to them, appears a bit dicey. What NVIDIA is doing here looks a lot like vendor financing, which is to say when your customer can’t afford to buy your products, you just provide them with the money to do so. The stock market cheered NVIDIA that day, pushing up its market cap by almost two-thirds of a Coca-Cola Company, or $170 billion.

As we write today, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) announced a deal whereby it will provide AI chips to power OpenAI’s next-generation AI infrastructure. AMD expects the deal to “deliver tens of billions of revenue,” though it didn’t say how long it would take to realize those revenues. As part of the deal, OpenAI has been granted a warrant for up to 160 million AMD shares (roughly 10% of the shares outstanding), structured to vest as specific milestones are achieved. The deal is already highly profitable for both companies, as AMD’s market value increased by $76 billion in the same day, or roughly one-third of a Coca-Cola Company.

We are asked frequently if all this AI hype is a bubble. As we already noted, the dollars being committed to AI infrastructure are dizzying, as are the market cap increases for those companies spending the money. It is unusual for companies committing massive amounts of capital to be instantly rewarded. Most large capital outlays are received with skepticism as management teams’ projections are often wrong. But not in the case of AI. Four big tech companies alone (Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta (META)) are collectively spending $350 billion to $400 billion in capex just this year, with much or most of it for building out AI-related compute capacity. We know this spending has excited investors, but is it actually generating a return on investment? The answer is complicated.

Let’s take a look at Alphabet, one of our long-standing holdings. We estimate Alphabet will spend $85 billion in capex this year. From 2021 through 2025, we estimate a cumulative spend of about $225 billion. How can we assess the return from this spending? Its EBIT was about $78 billion in 2021, and we believe it should end up around $135 billion this year. Clearly, the business continues to grow rapidly. But the business’s returns are falling. Alphabet’s return on capital employed (ROCE) was 74% pretax in 2021. In 2025, we estimate it will be 51% and at the margin, clearly much lower. Multiple factors are driving the returns down. The search business’s returns during its exponential growth phase were probably well over 100%. As it has matured and the company has continued to invest, returns have certainly fallen. New ventures such as Waymo are losing money.

YouTube and Google Cloud are both growing and are likely lower returning businesses than the legacy search. And, of course, the massive AI spend going into its cloud business and into building out Gemini is probably earning very low returns, if any. But large-scale capital investments don’t usually generate immediate returns. In the cloud business, Alphabet is adding capacity, which it will then sell. The capital is in the ground, and the revenue is on the come. Google Cloud is growing very rapidly and is probably worth half a trillion dollars now, according to some. (We could debate that valuation, certainly.) As for Gemini, it’s unclear how the revenue model will evolve. Currently, Alphabet sells Gemini subscriptions and uses Gemini to supplement search results through its Gemini AI summaries. We doubt Gemini subscriptions generate more than a few billion dollars of revenue, a drop in the bucket relative to Alphabet’s total revenue base and its capital spending.

Some portion of Alphabet’s capex spend is clearly defensive. The search business is probably worth $1.5 trillion to $2 trillion. Is it not rational to spend hundreds of billions to build its AI capabilities and defend itself from disruption from another AI competitor? Of course it is. So, as owners, we believe it is reasonable for Google to spend heavily on AI given its opportunity in cloud and its investment in Gemini, which may prove to be a large business at some point but is essential to defending the search business from disruption for now. But are the returns great on a standalone basis? For now, probably not.

Meta, another long-time holding, is similar. This year, the company is targeting capex investments around $70 billion, which is over 4X higher than what it spent in 2019. Over the past five years, its cumulative capex investments have totaled $180 billion. Over the same period, EBIT has increased 2.7X from $30 billion to $81 billion. Its ROCE has declined, but it remains exceptional in the high 30s. We estimate the incremental returns are in the 25%–30% range—a level well above the threshold for value creation (and a level that can only be described as aspirational for most companies). Similar to Google, it’s difficult to parse how much of the business’s growth was directly related to the increased investments. Certainly, many of its initiatives (e.g., Reality Labs and Meta AI glasses) are not generating any economic value yet. But Meta’s situation is unlike Google’s in one important way: Its AI spending should be largely incremental, driven by improving the core business and/or creating new business opportunities. It is likely to earn good returns by using AI to improve both content recommendations and the monetization of its core products through advertising. The returns from its investments in areas like Meta AI chatbots or AI glasses are less clear, but if successful, they should still be largely incremental to the core business.

Outside of the US tech industry, we don’t see much impact from AI at all. Companies talk a lot about how they are implementing, or trying to implement, AI into their operations to make them leaner, smarter and more profitable, of course. But we don’t see much actual impact. Margins don’t seem to be going up due to AI. Despite apocalyptic news stories, employees are not yet being made redundant at scale due to AI. Indeed, the consulting firm Accenture made headlines in September for 11,000 layoffs as part of its AI “reskilling” strategy, but a closer study shows that it is actually growing its ~800,000-person workforce this year.

Our research process is grounded in beliefs based on evidence. At this point, we can see the prospect for returns at some of the major technology companies, but there is little in terms of tangible impact across the industrial world. This could obviously change, but for now, the title of a recent Financial Times article sums up our view: “America’s top companies keep talking about AI—but can’t explain the upsides.”

We do believe that AI will prove to have a significant impact across industry and society, similar to other technological step changes, from the telegraph to the mobile phone. But given the amount of capital that is being committed and the amount of market value created in expectation of massive returns, the risks for disappointment between where we stand today and the eventual AI payoff are certainly not to be dismissed.

Portfolio Discussion

Our top-performing stocks this quarter were Alphabet (GOOG), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) and Lam Research (LRCX).

Alphabet shares rose 38% during the quarter. This represents a striking $810 billion increase in market value. Google’s core business continued to perform well, with accelerating revenue growth and strong profitability. The shares also certainly benefited from the general enthusiasm around AI. But the most significant performance driver was the favorable conclusion to the remedies phase of a DOJ antitrust trial. Recall that Google was deemed to have an illegal search monopoly in August 2024, and the DOJ was pushing for some draconian remedies that had the potential to impair large parts of Google’s business. After more than a year of this overhang, the court’s remedies announced this past September were quite reasonable.

There is an abundance of detail which we won’t go into, but the key findings revolve around divestitures, paying for default search placement and data sharing and syndication. The judge did not require Google to divest its home-grown Chrome browser and allowed Google to continue paying handset manufacturers, such as Apple, for default search engine placement. Some amount of data sharing and search syndication is required, but it’s limited. We think the judge ended up in largely the right place, since other outcomes would have had significant negative impacts on the technology ecosystem and consumer welfare.

Samsung shares rose 36% in the quarter, adding over $100 billion of market value. Samsung shares are finally starting to benefit from AI enthusiasm. The company is one of three major manufacturers of memory semiconductors, a key component in AI data centers. Samsung should have been at the center of the AI boom from the beginning, but it surprisingly fell behind on the technology curve and failed to develop cutting-edge chips capable of meeting the demanding specifications of the biggest AI customers. But Samsung started to catch up this quarter, announcing that Tesla will purchase $16.5 billion of chips from Samsung’s Texas foundry. The deal marks a major proof point for its foundry strategy, which had thus far been slow to gain traction. Samsung also demonstrated progress on developing a high-bandwidth memory (HBM) product that it should be able to sell into NVIDIA’s supply chain. If NVIDIA accepts the product, Samsung’s semiconductor business should be significantly more profitable next year. In addition, the massive demand from AI has created a favorable pricing environment for commodity memory chips, where Samsung is still the leading player.

Lam Research shares rose 38% during the quarter. The reason should come as no surprise—Lam is one of the most direct beneficiaries of the AI data center investment frenzy. The company is one of four leading makers of semiconductor manufacturing equipment, which are the machines used to produce advanced semiconductors. This positions Lam well to benefit from the massive investments in AI data centers in the coming years. Further, Lam’s particular specialty is equipment for making memory semiconductors. Memory chips are in short supply due to the strong demand from AI, which will require meaningful capacity expansion to meet expected demand. All of this should lead to strong demand for Lam’s products and has driven the share price up nearly 100% this year. Like many other parts of the market, Lam’s valuation reflects its favorable outlook, with shares trading at over 30X earnings.

Our worst performing stocks were Elevance (ELV), Aramark (ARMK) and Henry Schein (HSIC).

Elevance’s share price declined by 16%, making it our largest negative contributor. It was also our worst performer in Q2, and we wrote extensively about the stock’s woes at that time. Without going over the same ground again, we can simply say that about half of Elevance’s premiums, or $100 billion, are currently not making any margin. This is due to the upheaval in its Medicaid and the Affordable Care Act (ACA) business. The Medicaid market is under unprecedented profit pressure due to loss cost trend well above premium rate growth and a meaningful deterioration in the risk pool due to the reverification impact post COVID-19. These pressures may get worse in the near term as the recently passed One Big Beautiful Bill adds work verification requirements that may further disrupt the risk pool. All Medicaid insurers are aggressively lobbying state sponsors of Medicaid for rate increases which are, in our mind, a question of when, not if. States also appear to be changing the benefit designs of their plans in order to bend the cost curve.

The ACA has also emerged as a margin headwind. Tighter income verification practices have churned off large numbers of enrollees, especially the healthy ones, who are typically high-margin participants. The risk pools have deteriorated with consequent impact on margins. Managed care companies are responding by increasing rates in order to recover margins, which will likely shrink the pool of members further. At the end of 2025, the enhanced ACA plan subsidies are scheduled to expire, which will further pressure the risk pool, though these subsidies may get extended via legislation.

Democrats in the Senate are using this issue as leverage, refusing to pass the continuing resolution to fund the government unless these subsidies are extended.

We expect Medicaid to revert to more normal margins over the next year or two, and we expect the ACA business to be profitable with a smaller number of enrollees and a lower base of premium revenue. Elevance stock is very cheap, and we have added to our holdings over the past six-month period of share price decline.

Aramark shares declined 8% during the quarter. There is nothing particularly notable about the performance this quarter. Aramark was one of our top performers last quarter, and it gave back some of those gains. The business is performing quite well, with good organic growth and profitability. The outlook for next year is similarly favorable, with continued growth and margin improvements. We believe the shares are reasonably priced at 20X earnings.

Henry Schein was down 9% this quarter. The dental market has been stagnant this year, with low growth and customer price sensitivity. In addition, the company is still struggling with weak profitability due to a bloated cost structure. The combination of slow growth and high costs has made it hard for Henry Schein to grow profits. The good news is that we are optimistic about recent changes at the board and management level. Earlier this year, KKR became the largest shareholder and joined the board. Shortly afterward, longtime CEO Stanley Bergman announced his retirement. Both are welcome changes. The company has a long track record of value creation but has struggled operationally over the past 4–5 years, and we believe that new management, in partnership with KKR, should help it address these recent operational issues.

Henry Schein shares trade at 13X earnings. The company is the leader in a defensive and stable market, and we believe its earnings should grow in the high-single digits over time due to market growth, improved product mix and cost reductions. The opportunity to buy a market leader in a good industry at a modest multiple is rare in this market.

data.title: Cost Discipline And Backlog Wins To Offset DMC Global's Headwinds
data.description:
BOOM Repositions For Long-Cycle Growth

I expect DMC Global (BOOM) to step up on the growth path, despite the 25% decline over the past year. It also underperformed the VanEck Oil Services ETF (OIH) in this period. What ails the recovery of its DynaEnergetics segment is a combination of US land completion activity weakness and typical Q4 energy-market exhaustion. The US onshore drilling and completions activity is softening, led by weaker oil prices and OPEC+ supply push.

Investors may note that the DynaEnergetics segment deals in perforating systems, while NobelClad produces clad metal plates for LNG equipment. A deterioration in the energy environment can affect the BOOM’s DynaEnergetics and NobelClad segments. But we have assured revenue visibility into 2026 from robust backlog growth in Q3. That should allay the fears, at least to a large extent.

I also have concerns over the commercial and high-end residential demand, especially in an uncertain economic environment. Margins, however, should get traction as the management is tightening costs across all segments. Its relative valuation remains below peers, offering upside if EBITDA grows modestly. Backed by higher cash flows, I recommend maintaining the “Hold” call, unchanged from my previous iteration.

End Market Drivers

BOOM’s Arcadia segment results are tied to commercial and high-end residential buildings. Over the past year, the new privately owned housing units declined by 6%. The fall was especially sharp in August 2025. The frac spread count decreased by 14% year-to-date. Demand growth uncertainty and geopolitical concerns have caused market vulnerability in 2025.

Segment Outlook

In the current environment, I see that the DynaEnergetics segment in North America remains under pressure from tariffs and weaker completion activity. It will likely face the usual late-quarter seasonal dip. Arcadia, on the other hand, should hold its own as home building stabilizes. Overall, I think the outlook carries macro risk. But the management is clear that shifting energy markets and tariff swings could push results in either direction. I lean into this dichotomy because I see that US frac spreads have declined by 14% year to date. On the other hand, deepwater and long-cycle, which offer better breakevens and longer reserve lives, present offsetting growth opportunities.

In Q4, BOOM’s management expects sales to land between $140 million and $150 million, or nearly unchanged from Q3. Its adjusted EBITDA can stay within a range of $5 million to $8 million, which would be a 39% fall from Q3. Backlog, however, was strong. It ended the quarter at $57 million, up 53% from Q2, even before adding the follow-on order. NobelClad secured a $20 million order for a petrochemical project in Q3. The order has an option to add another $5 million. This, according to the company, marks one of the most significant awards in several years.

Strategy Updates

BOOM is repositioning Arcadia, DynaEnergetics, and NobelClad through operational improvements, right-sizing, and backlog rebuilding. In Arcadia, it is right-sizing high-end residential offerings. In NobleClad, it looks to maintain operational discipline. So, it can pursue this rather than focusing on production capacity. But its focus goes beyond the income statement. It prioritizes disciplined capital allocation to support margin expansion and cash-flow generation. So, it will tighten its capital structure to improve resilience.

It is paying down debt and shrinking net leverage. The company’s net debt has declined by 47% since the start of the year. It also looks to improve cash generation to fund operations instead of relying on credit. Investors may note that the company’s revolver facility remains undrawn, giving it a cushion during soft quarters in the U.S. onshore.

Near-Term Challenges

It looks like any seasonal slowdown will hit the DynaEnergetics margins because it will stay under pricing pressure, and lower volumes won’t cover fixed manufacturing costs. NobelClad faces the same issue. While the order volume growth has been impressive, it won’t ship until 2026, leaving several quarters of softer absorption.

My Growth Estimates

Over the past 13 quarters, BOOM’s adjusted EBITDA increased by 13% on average. The margin is at a crossroads as opposing forces keep it grounded. While its backlog strength can pull revenues higher, I expect its EBITDA to stay relatively steady due to the challenges I already discussed. I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase by 6%-8% over the next four quarters.

Segment And Margin Performances In Q3

BOOM’s Q3 earnings report was released on November 4. From Q2 to Q3, NobelClad segment revenues decreased by 21%. I would attribute the fall to softer bookings from earlier quarters and reduced volumes. As volumes shrank, fixed-cost absorption got limited and pulled margins lower. Revenues from the DynaEnergetics segment increased by 3%, while revenues from the Arcadia segment remained unchanged.

If we look closely, Arcadia’s adjusted EBITDA margin improved in Q3. The expansion came from steadier execution and better absorption of fixed costs as stabilization efforts finally yielded results. DynaEnergetics, however, was weighed down by weaker U.S. onshore activity, lower pricing, and tariff-related cost pressure, leading to a lower EBITDA margin in Q3.

Cash Flows And Balance Sheet

In 9M 2025, BOOM's cash flow from operations, despite lower revenues, increased by 10% compared to a year ago. Free cash flow increased by 18% during this period.

BOOM's net debt was $30 million as of September 30, 2025. Its leverage (debt-to-equity) of 0.22x improved (from 0.28x) at the end of FY2024. The current leverage is also lower than many of its peers.

Relative Valuation And Target Price

BOOM’s forward EV/EBITDA expansion contrasts with its peers (like CLB, HAL, and OIS). That basically means investors are expecting BOOM’s earnings to contract next year, which usually leads to a much lower valuation multiple. Right now, BOOM’s EV/EBITDA is sitting at 3.6x, while its peers average around 8.6x. So, compared to the group, NOA looks to be trading at a reasonable price.

Looking back over the past five years, BOOM’s average multiple was 5.3x. And if it somehow trades at the peer average of 8.6x, we’re talking about a 2.3x upside. As I mentioned earlier, I’m expecting adjusted EBITDA to grow by 4%–6% over the next year. If we plug those numbers into the EV formula and use the current 3.6x multiple, the stock should be trading somewhere between $6.3 and $6.5—which would be around a 14% bump from here.

Analyst Rating

According to Seeking Alpha, one sell-side analyst rated BOOM a "Buy" (including a "Strong Buy"). Two rated it a "Hold," and none rated it a "Sell." The consensus target price is $10.3, which implies an 82% upside at the current price. Given the short-term obstacles to margin growth, I think the sell-side analysts are overestimating the returns from the stock.

What's My Take On BOOM?

Here is what I see in the environment: U.S. onshore remains weak. Offshore and international work continues to build momentum. So, BOOM’s DynaEnergetics segment stays under pressure from tariffs and softer completion activity. The company has activated its strategic activities by reshaping all operating segments through cost resets, tighter capital allocation, and a lighter balance sheet.

By Q3, NobelClad’s significant petrochemical order has added strong visibility. But I do not see the company realizing the benefits until 2026. As a result, its Q4 revenue should be flat. EBITDA, however, can recover partially as the added backlog offers some insulation. I think the seasonal dips and poor absorption remain near-term risks. To its advantage, net debt has dropped sharply this year. And, valuation screens low relative to peers. Considering all the variables, I continue to consider BOOM a “Hold.”

data.title: Enghouse Systems: Intriguing M&A Strategy But Not Enough Yet
data.description:
Enghouse Systems Limited (ENGH:CA) is a software acquirer and integrator company. They focus on vertical markets like communications, transportation, and public safety. Its structure is broadly organized in two segments: 1) the Interactive Management Group (IMG) for contact center, video, and client-engagement tools, and 2) the Asset Management Group for telecom network software, transportation systems, and emergency solutions. Essentially, ENGH purchases profitable companies with recurring income, and then enhances their technology for integrations like adding cloud and AI features. However, for the most part, I don’t think there’s a very compelling opportunity with ENGH at these levels, which is why I deem it a “Hold” for now.

Cloud And AI Rollups

Enghouse Systems Limited is a software company that acquires and develops vertical platforms for communications, transportation, and public-safety solutions. ENGH was founded back in 1984 and is currently headquartered in Markham, Ontario, Canada, with offices in 25 countries. ENGH also grows organically by simply pursuing more software and services clients, while at the same time it seeks opportunistic M&A in complementary software companies to grow repeat revenues.

As a quick overview, ENGH has two business segments. The first one is the Interactive Management Group (IMG), which handles their contact center, video, and marketing solutions. The other segment is Asset Management Group (AMG) for networks, transportation, and public safety.

Moreover, ENGH’s portfolio includes interactive systems that improve customer navigation through automation, visibility in several channels, and analytics. Its solutions cover customer experience (CX), communications, and AI-driven engagement tools, with several categories such as Omni-Channels. This software integrates phone, chat, email, video, and social media in a single interface for agents, with products such as Contact Center as a Service (CCaaS), AI-powered social media management, and secure video.

Another product category within IMG’s solutions is call reception for enterprise-level attendant consoles. This software category includes video interaction, with AI-powered chatbots, and quality management to monitor and analyze agent performance. These solutions provide observations for optimization using AI, and they can offer a bridge to CRM systems. Additionally, IMG provides niche specialty solutions, such as marketing-communication tools, mobile device management, and telecom-expense management.

As for ENGH’s AMG segment, they manage the company’s network portfolio that provides telecom infrastructure and monetization platforms. Its solutions include Business Support Systems (BSS), with Billing-as-a-Service, IoT monetization, and other tools. These services generally help telecoms generate their revenues, which is why I consider ENGH’s role as a third-party B2B service provider of sorts. In addition, AMG offers network infrastructure to manage data and voice traffic, operation support systems (OSS), and platforms for media and TV-as-a-Service.

Growth Through M&A

Interestingly, ENGH’s AMG segment also provides a transportation and public safety portfolio to support operations with mobility, logistics, and emergency systems. Its products cover transit operations and payments for public transportation. On top of that, ENGH covers control systems for first responders, communication tools for operators and dispatchers in logistics, and even fleet management for tracking vehicles and monitoring their performance. This is how ENGH has built, over time, an impressive rollup portfolio of smaller companies. Their latest investor presentation mentioned ENGH typically targets acquisitions in the $5 million to $50 million revenue range.

They also appear to favor acquisitions that are cash flow accretive, with a payback period of 5 to 7 years. But what also stands out is that the AMG portion of their business is quickly becoming their main value driver. You see, ENGH generated CAD $125.6 million in revenues during Q3 2025, which was slightly down 3.8% YoY. However, the AMG segment itself grew 5.6% YoY and contributed CAD $56.0 million in sales. And within AMG, transportation and public safety are increasingly playing a larger role, which management believes is synergistic with their broader SaaS strategy.

Therefore, ENGH’s growth comes from buying existing software companies with proven vertical solutions by targeting recurring revenue, mission-critical applications, and high barriers to entry. And, even though most of ENGH’s products came through M&A, they also have in-house R&D. For example, the company’s engineering teams integrate and develop any acquired software and add features for interoperability or cloud migration. ENGH also does R&D in essential areas like video compression (from Vidyo), network monitoring, and customer service tools leveraging AI.

So, ENGH is not just a holding company that accumulates subsidiaries. They seem to have a more active operational role, mostly focusing on creating synergies throughout their portfolio of companies by sharing all of their technology as appropriate. This know-how facilitates adding value to the solutions provided by the acquired companies. In that sense, in 2025, ENGH acquired three noteworthy software companies. These were Acculab, Margento, and Trafi. Acculab develops telephone and voice-technology solutions, Margento produces mobile payment and smart-ticketing solutions for transport and fintech applications, and Trafi is a Mobility-as-a-Service platform that connects public transport and mobility systems.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, ENGH trades at a CAD $1.1 billion market cap (or $786.2 million). Their latest quarterly financials (July 2025) show their balance sheet holds $196.0 million in cash and short-term investments. They don’t have external financial debt, only regular operating liabilities. And their book value reached $435.0 million, indicating a reasonable P/B of 1.8.

Moreover, according to Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on ENGH, the company is projected to reach CAD $513.1 million in revenues by 2026 (or $363.4 million). This would price ENGH at a forward P/S of just 2.1. And I estimate that ENGH generated $19.2 million in quarterly cash flows as of July 2025. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX. And if we annualize that number, it indicates a yearly cash flow of roughly $76.8 million. In other words, ENGH’s market cap is around 10.2x its annual CF run rate.

That’s why, generally speaking, I think ENGH seems to be about fairly valued at this point. However, I feel I’m not quite sure it fully justifies its risks. For example, I previously mentioned that it’s only its AMG category that’s actually growing. If you look at the IMG segment, this part of their business actually contracted by 10.2% YoY. And, while this is not necessarily a major red flag, it’s still an issue that I think deserves a slightly deeper discount. After all, ENGH’s management frames their strategy as “two-pronged.” But clearly, one of those prongs is declining significantly, which I would say makes it a much riskier investment for long-term investors.

Similarly, the stock itself has been disappointing during 2025, and it's down 24% YTD. And while their dividend yield of 5.8% is attractive on paper, this hasn’t been enough to offset the sell-off in the share price itself. Still, I concede that ENGH has consistently paid a quarterly dividend over the years, which may make it more suitable for income investors. And the quarterly dividend has historically grown over time as well, suggesting management considers this policy a priority.

Besides, as of July 2025, they had 55.1 million shares outstanding, so the current dividend per share of CAD $0.30 represents a cash outflow of roughly CAD $16.5 million per quarter (or $11.7 million). And I think that’s manageable compared to my quarterly cash flow estimate of $19.2 million. Lastly, the stock’s trading volume seems decent with 392.1 thousand shares. At the current CAD $20.00 PPS, it implies a trading liquidity of approximately CAD $7.8 million (or $5.5 million). Hence, despite ENGH being a foreign issuer, slippage costs shouldn’t be a major issue for most retail investors.

Conclusion: Mixed Bag

Overall, I think ENGH has a bit of mixed investment profile. On one hand, I feel their consistent dividends and compelling yield could make it suitable for income investors. And after their recent stock decline, ENGH doesn’t seem wildly overvalued either. However, I also believe ENGH has some serious issues given that one of their strategic prongs (i.e., IMG) is rapidly contracting. Plus, I don’t see a really unique business moat or competitive advantage with ENGH’s underlying prospects. That’s why, on balance, I ultimately lean neutral on the stock at these levels. In my view, ENGH is the type of stock that can make sense at a much deeper discount, but for now, it doesn’t seem like an overly exciting opportunity.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rezolve AI PLC: I'd Like To See How The Company Plans To Grow Its ARR To $500m
data.description:
Introduction

Rezolve AI PLC (RZLV) has been doing the rounds recently online, which prompted me to take a look at it in more detail to see what it is all about. The company’s revenue growth has been tremendous and is projected to grow much more in the next year; however, I am a little skeptical of such projections, as they truly seem to be quite unbelievable, so I would like to see more details in the upcoming earnings calls to get a better sense of where the company is heading. Additionally, the company’s share price may again be picked up by a future meme frenzy, which poses extra risks and overvaluation.

However, I do see potential and will be following it along to see how this story plays out.

The Company

Rezolve AI PLC is not to be confused with Resolve AI, which is a completely different company (they should probably do something about it). It is a pure-play AI company that is looking to transform how retail commerce can increase customer engagement through AI-driven search, product discovery, personalized shopping experiences, and checkout solutions. The company aims to help retailers with what they call the best salesperson, their AI, make sales through recommending the best product that the person needs, with much more refined search options and offerings that help retain more customers and keep them coming back.

The company monetizes through a B2B2C approach that targets large e-commerce enterprises, merchants, and payment service providers. The company uses a SaaS business model, charging a monthly fixed fee, with a key operating metric being annual recurring revenue, or ARR for short.

The company’s products include a “Brain Suite” of AI solutions, specifically Brain Commerce, which aims to enhance search accuracy and product discovery, and Brain Checkout, which helps with digital payments and frictionless transactions. These solutions drive increased conversion rates, higher average order values, and customer satisfaction.

The company spiked in popularity recently due to explosive growth in enterprise adoption, having over 100 global clients, like H&M (OTCPK:HNNMY), ASOS Plc (OTCPK:ASOMF), and Urban Outfitters (URBN), as well as notable partnerships with Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet (GOOG) that provide cloud infrastructure and co-selling programs.

Briefly on Financials

RZLV recently surprised analysts by beating sales estimates by around $1m, which grew over 425% y/y, coming in at around $6.3m. The company’s Brain Suite products, like Brain Commerce and Brain Checkout, are now live with over 100 enterprise customers around the globe, which helped the company secure over $90m in ARR.

The company is still in its early growth stage, which means it is very unprofitable right now, with G&A expenses ballooning to over $33m, bringing in an operating loss of -$32m for the six months ended June 2025. Additionally, the company’s bottom line suffered even more due to a loss on extinguishment, although these are non-cash charges tied to debt conversions and restructuring. Nevertheless, the company’s GAAP loss came in at around -$0.25. The company’s gross margins for the same period came in at a whopping 95.8% vs. the consensus range of 60%-70%. It seems that analysts don’t overestimate how much the cloud-based SaaS business model requires to run. There’s hardly any cost of sales once the software is delivered to the customer and set up via the cloud. There is very little upkeep and low incremental costs per additional customer. Once everything is set up, the only costs will be compute, storage, and bandwidth, which are not that expensive.

Looking at the company’s financial position, as of the end of June, the company’s cash position stood at around $9.8m, and looking at the company’s debt, most of which is due within a year, it was around $30m. Since the report came out, the company raised $200m in oversubscribed financing, which will easily cover the company’s debt and will help it continue to operate at a loss for a little longer. The company’s cash burn rate is substantial, so future financing arrangements will be required if operations don’t improve dramatically. For the first half of the year, the company’s cash flow was negative $20m, which means that at this rate, there is around $3.3m cash burn per month, giving the company over 5 years of runway. I'm sure that the cash burn rate may continue to increase, so let's call it 2-3 years by being a bit more conservative.

The management emphasized, in the recent conference call, that while reported losses appear high, they are mostly accounting charges and one-offs, so mostly not actual cash charges.

Additionally, with the successful $200m equity financing, the management said that it is confident the company has plenty of "liquidity to support its accelerated scale-up and partnership-driven strategy".

On the same call, the company mentioned a little about its cash burn situation, and just as I said that the cash burn will most likely rise, the management is of the same view, saying, "We anticipate the ongoing cash burn will continue—and potentially rise in the near term—as we invest in team growth, global enterprise expansion, and securing high-value contracts to drive ARR."

Overall, it does seem like quite a risky investment based on the company’s early finances; however, if the company succeeds at attracting more enterprises that are willing to use the company’s proprietary product to enhance customer retention and engagement, these numbers could drastically improve.

Comments on the Outlook

Massive Growth Ahead

A lot of investors are very bullish on the company now that the management said the company will end the year with around $150m in ARR, which is quite an improvement. What is even more impressive is that the management said that the company will achieve around $500m ARR exit rate for 2026. The enterprise partners seem to really like RZLV’s product, and such an increase in ARR in just one year really tells us that the company has been very active in trying to sign up a lot of different enterprises and expand its market reach.

The company is due to report soon; however, there is still no solid date set. I would like to see what kind of numbers the company is going to put up next and whether we will actually see $150m in ARR for this year. If we do, then I would have an easier time believing the company can achieve $500m in ARR for 2026. I am a little bit skeptical right now, but who knows, maybe the company’s product actually helps these enterprises with customer retention and experience that makes them shop more and come back much more often than not.

With such a projected increase in ARR, the company will have to announce quite a few massive partnerships. The company said it anticipates announcing a lot more new partnerships and collaborations globally. With the collaborations with Google and Microsoft under its belt and the recent $10m contract with Mexico's Liverpool department store chain, the company is paving the way to move across the globe, and I expect the company not to lose momentum.

The company, with such a portfolio of companies behind it, has a decent leg to stand on, which demonstrates that there is potential, which is valuable for these department store chains and the like. I wouldn't be surprised if the company announces some massive partnerships with some huge retail store chains in the future in the US, Mexico, and Europe, since they already have a foothold in these regions. The execution of it will be vital for the company to sustain its operations.

One of the company’s product features is its Agentic AI, which is becoming quite a big buzz around the world of technology. I am currently using an agentic AI for some app building, and I find the overall experience decent, although maybe slightly expensive, especially since, after using it for many months now, I find it to be not as good as I hoped. Oftentimes, the AI agent would get stuck in a sort of a loop and not provide me with any reasonable solution, which in turn cost me money. If the chat window has been open for too long, it could start misbehaving and offer solutions out of thin air with a back of how they got there.

Rezolve’s brainpowa AI engine is claimed to achieve near 0% hallucination, and I, for one, find this to be questionable. This is a common challenge in general for LLMs like ChatGPT and Gemini. How is the company managing such a feat? Well, from what I gathered, the engine is trained on specific eCommerce data, with domain-specific data focused strictly on retail product information and transactional context. They continue to fine-tune and validate against real-world retail scenarios to correct any inaccuracies, as well as they incorporate built-in verification layers that cross-check AI responses with the verified product databases. Since it is being trained on a lot more specific data, I can see the hallucination rates being this low. It has been working out so far, and it would be interesting to see what happens when the company enters into a partnership with a much bigger retailer.

In terms of the company’s ability to grow, seeing such healthy, near 100% gross margins, I can see there is a lot of potential to scale the business going forward. These types of margins will allow the company to become profitable once the aggressive growth phase subsides or once the company reaches a lot more in ARR. If the company reaches $500m in ARR by the end of 2026, would this be enough to finally turn a profit? At least on non-GAAP terms? It’s hard to tell given the massive growth in operating expenses right now, but if ARR continues to grow like this, there is a huge possibility the company will turn positive on paper.

What Worries Me Right Now

The biggest risks that I can see right now are some operational and some social.

Operationally speaking, since the company is being very aggressive in its growth right now, expenses are piling up, the company is deeper in the red than before, and the need to continue to finance this growth massively dilutes current shareholders. If we look at the most recent report, we can see that the company’s share count went from around 151m in June ’24 to 233m in June ’25. This is before the company secured an additional $200m in oversubscribed financing, which means there are going to be a lot more shares on the following report. Looking at the 6-K form for the offering, the company is issuing 37m shares at $5.40 per share. It is a massive dilution, but I think it is necessary, especially in such early stages. The company’s share price appreciation could easily outweigh the share dilution, but if that doesn’t materialize, then it is a real risk.

Potential Meme Stock

I’ve been lurking around Reddit’s (RDDT) many subreddits regarding finance and stocks and the like, and there have been quite a few times that I have seen Rezolve’s name popping up. I believe just yesterday I saw a person going all in on RZLV with little to no DD, other than the whole “I like the stock” shtick. These types of posts are common and, I must say, are quite entertaining, but they are not good for a company that is not trying to look like a meme and is trying to grow as a real business. The recent Beyond Meat (BYND) drama spawned many subreddits and cult-like behavior, which grew old real fast, and yet there are still rumblings of diamond hands for BYND stock. Rezolve could see yet another spike in its share price, as it did back in late August to mid-September. Since then, the company’s share price has plummeted, which will most likely lead to another meme frenzy in the near future, especially when the company is going to report its numbers.

Investor Takeaway

In my view, there is something here, if all the projected numbers are to be believed. The company is experiencing fantastic growth, but that isn’t coming without risks. If the company cannot convert more enterprises into using the company’s products while its expenses continue to balloon, further share price dilution to finance the company’s operations is inevitable.

I am going to hold off on recommending the company to buy at this stage, mainly because the claims of such ARR are a little hard to believe, and I would like to see this story come closer to the truth. The upcoming quarter could give us a better glimpse into the company’s future outlook regarding new partnerships that are going to fuel such growth in ARR.

I would like to see this company forgotten by the many subreddits so that the company’s share would better align with its operations.

Additionally, I already own a company in my portfolio that is rather risky and may be seen as even more risky than RZLV since it is still a pre-revenue company, so I am not looking to add another high-risk play for now but will be following closely to see how this story develops.

data.title: Bank Of Korea Easing May Be Over Amid Improved Growth Outlook
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

Today’s meeting sent strong signals the BoK’s rate-cutting cycle has come to an end

The odds of further Bank of Korea easing seem low amid upgraded forecasts for South Korea’s GDP and inflation. Increased inflation concerns, risks linked to the weak KRW, and ongoing financial instability were all reflected in changes to the BoK’s forward guidance in the policy meeting statement.

In its forward guidance, the BoK removed the phrase “maintain rate cut stance” and instead added that it is “leaving room for potential rate cuts.” In our view, the BoK intends to convey that its easing cycle remains ongoing rather than concluded to mitigate the risk of abrupt movements in financial markets. Also, they appear to be closely monitoring any sudden change in market rates that may signal an impending rate hike.

Governor Rhee stated that it’s premature to consider a rate hike at this time, emphasising that changes in policy direction generally require a 12-month implementation period.

Additionally, today's decision was not unanimous. There was one dissenting vote, the same as the last meeting. Opinions on a rate cut in the next three months remain divided, and support for easing has dropped from four to three. Yet the idea has not been abandoned.

Nevertheless, it is anticipated that changes in macroeconomic conditions will prompt the BoK to end its easing stance.

The BoK have revised up its GDP and CPI outlook

The BoK expects the economy to grow 1.8% year on year (vs 1.6% previously) in 2026 and 1.9% in 2027. Strong chips and the IT cycle will boost growth more than previously expected. Stronger-than-expected third-quarter GDP is the main reason for the upward revision of 2025 GDP outlook to 1.0% (vs 0.9% previously).

In our view, the revision was rather moderate. We expect GDP to rise by 1.2% in 2025 and 2.0% in 2026 amid stronger-than-expected private consumption and exports. If upcoming data is solid, in line with our expectations, the BoK will revise up its outlook again next year.

CPI inflation for 2026 has been revised up from 1.9% to 2.1%, likely due to recent KRW weakness, which has driven higher-than-expected inflation. A 2.1% rate inflation isn’t a big deviation from the BoK’s target level, and thus shouldn’t be a major concern.

GDP/CPI outlook : ING vs BoK

Concerns over household credit and high housing prices continued

Strict government controls on mortgages and home buying are gradually slowing the housing market, but underlying demand remains strong. The recent consumer sentiment survey showed that expectations for housing prices dropped but remain elevated. Thus, the BoK will have to monitor the housing market trends. We anticipate that the housing market will stabilise, at least in the short term, as the government considers implementing stricter measures. Nevertheless, this is unlikely to provide sufficient confidence for the BoK to lower the policy rate.

The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Janus Henderson Global Research Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

  • Global equities rose strongly in U.S. dollar terms on easing trade tensions and signs of resilient economic growth in many countries.
  • U.S. market performance was especially strong, aided by expectations for Federal Reserve (Fed) interest rate cuts. The Fed reduced policy rates in September, with additional rate cuts expected by year-end.
  • The market advance was relatively narrow, with notable gains in information technology stocks that rose on excitement around artificial intelligence (AI). The more defensive consumer staples sector detracted from relative performance.

Portfolio review

Relative performance was hindered by an investment in Intuit (INTU). The company’s enterprise software products include Quickbooks, an accounting, payroll, and payments solution for small- and mid-sized businesses, and TurboTax, a leading tax preparation software. The stock rallied earlier in the year on strong results for the tax software business, particularly for TurboTax Live, which combines tax software, human experts, and AI capabilities. Intuit also reported strong results for its mid-market Quickbooks business. Following this performance, shares of Intuit sold off in the third quarter on investor concerns about its guidance for fiscal year 2026. This weaker guidance reflected headwinds for Intuit’s Mailchimp business, rather than for its core Quickbooks business. Investors have also been concerned about how generative AI may impact software businesses that use Google Search to drive spending. We believe the stock sell-off was overdone, and we remain invested as we are encouraged by the recent performance of TurboTax and Quickbooks.

Progressive (PGR) was also a relative detractor. The insurer has been a strong performer in recent years, as robust policy growth and improving underwriting profitability in the personal automobile insurance market supported earnings expansion. The stock lagged in the third quarter, however, due to investor concerns about the sustainability of its strong policy growth and pricing power. Insurance stocks, more broadly, have also detracted from relative results in a market focused on AI opportunities and pro-cyclical financials. On a positive note, Progressive’s underwriting profitability remained strong. Growth has also persisted, though at a slower pace relative to recent years. We believe Progressive remains well positioned to gain market share due to its competitive advantages in scale, risk segmentation, and distribution.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) (TSM) was a relative contributor. As one of the world's dominant chip manufacturers, TSMC is the leading supplier of graphic processing units to companies, such as NVIDIA, that are at the forefront of the AI buildout. Excitement around the AI supply chain grew as leading hyperscalers and other technology companies continued to announce substantive investments in high performance computing. We believe TSMC is well positioned to capitalize on this spending due to its unparalleled scale, deep technological advantages, and its partnerships with major semiconductor companies and hyperscalers. The company has further expanded its market leadership as competitors have stumbled or shifted away from the foundry business, leaving TSMC as the unparallel market leader in this space.

Lam Research (LRCX), another contributor, manufactures specialized deposition and etching tools critical to producing the advanced chips needed for AI. Lam has become a structural share gainer in wafer fabrication equipment (WFE) because of both its strong customer relationships and rising etch and deposition intensity across leading-edge foundry/logic, DRAM, and advanced packaging. Shares of LAM rose as the company exceeded its revenue and earnings target, driven by strength across business lines and market share gains in the WFE market. Operational improvements also supported margin improvement. We see long-term opportunity for Lam Research as a market leader in many of the technologies that enable AI.

Manager outlook

The market has continued to rally on strong corporate earnings news and relatively stable U.S. economic growth. Investors have also moved away from their worst-case fears about U.S. trade policy, which has boosted the performance of risk assets. The Fed has started to lower policy rates, which may help reduce funding costs while providing a renewed tailwind for some market sectors, such as residential construction. Additionally, we have been encouraged by positive economic news outside of the U.S., especially in Europe, which may offer opportunities for companies with international exposure.

As we continue to monitor shifting macro and policy currents and their impact on the companies, we remain committed to our bottom-up, fundamentally driven investment process. The foundation of this process is our exceptional team of analysts, who bring deep knowledge of their respective sectors. Our analyst team is constantly on the lookout for companies with strong or improving business models, exceptional management teams, and underappreciated competitive advantages. Through our time-tested, disciplined investment approach, we will continue to pursue our goal of long-term growth in capital.

data.title: Coca-Cola: Strong Quarter, Stronger Margins - But Limited Upside
data.description:
The reading I made about Coca-Cola (KO) in my previous article is reinforced after analyzing in detail the results of Q3 2025. At this point, KO gives me the feeling of being a company operating almost on autopilot, but in the best sense, since it operates the business with millimeter precision even when the global context is very heterogeneous and when consumption dynamics vary region by region.

What strikes me most in reviewing this quarter is not the growth itself, which was solid, but the consistency. KO grew back in line with the high of its structural goal, defended margins even with more marketing investment, sustained volume, and continued to gain global share. In short, I confirmed what I already sensed, and that is that the competitive advantage of the company not only remains intact but seems to be strengthening.

However, as I pointed out in September, the problem lies not in execution but in the valuation that the market validates by this predictability. Today, KO continues to trade with multiples above its historical averages and a considerable premium against the sector. So, although I value the stability it offers, I see little room for expansion of multiples at these prices. In this scenario, I maintain my hold posture.

Q3 2025: Fine Execution, Solid Organic Growth, and Surprising Margins Again

According to Q3 results, net operating revenues grew 5% YoY to $12.5B, and organic revenue rose 6% YoY, driven by a price mix of 6% and a volume that increased 1%. Since the conference, they mentioned that the quarter started slower, but September brought an acceleration that allowed them to comply with the operational guidance without any problems. When I see this combination (stable or slightly positive volume plus sustained pricing), it is when I perceive the strength of the portfolio.

The management summed it up clearly:

We are confident we can deliver on our 2025 guidance while also working to achieve our longer term objectives.

Earnings confirmed that reading. Comparable EPS was $0.82, up 6% YoY despite a negative FX impact of also 6%, and diluted EPS for the quarter was $0.86. For me, these results show that KO managed to turn a correct quarter into a very solid one precisely because it was able to expand profits in a context that did not play completely in its favor.

Where I most notice the quality of business is on the margins. Comparable gross margin was close to 61%, with an underlying expansion of around 80 bps YoY, while the comparable operating margin was 31.87%, advancing about 115 bps YoY. And the most interesting data, in my opinion, is the expansion of the underlying operating margin (~260 bps), which reflects the combination of organic growth, efficiency, and cost control. It strikes me that this is happening while KO continues to increase its investment in marketing, something that demonstrates operational discipline.

In short, what I see is continuity. Coca-Cola not only grows but also turns that growth into higher profitability quarter after quarter, and that is a clear sign of a company with a mature but not stagnant model.

Performance by Region: A Portfolio That Resists Almost Any Scenario

At this point in the analysis, when I review region by region, what interests me most is not only the number of points but also how KO reacts to each context. And in North America that was very clear. While volumes remained stable, there was strong growth in revenue and profits. What struck me most was the way in which they adjusted the commercial execution, with more equipment placement, better product availability, and very fine work on pricing per channel. These tactical decisions, which are not always seen in the headlines, explain why KO continues to gain share even in developed markets.

In Latin America, however, the results were more varied. Brazil showed a very solid performance, Chile and Colombia remained stable, and Mexico continued to show the macro weakness already expected. Even so, KO regained value share in the region and maintained positive organic revenue at constant currency. For me, this reinforces something that I have been seeing for a long time, and it is that, even with a complicated macro, the system knows how to defend pricing and volume in a surgical way.

On the other hand, Europe and EMEA had a better quarter than the environment suggested. Europe faced demanding bases, but KO still managed to grow revenues and results with campaigns linked to major events and precise management of price architecture. In the Middle East and Africa, volume directly grew despite a volatile macro, which I think is one of the most telling indicators of portfolio strength. When a company performs well in unstable markets, it tends to perform even better in stable ones.

Asia-Pacific had a more mixed dynamic. India, China, and the Philippines were affected by adverse climate, lower consumption, and sectoral weakness, while Japan and Australia showed much more stable performance. This mixture generated a composition effect that artificially inflated the average price, but without changing the real trend... There is growth in this region, yes, but it is not homogeneous.

And when I look at all the information together, undoubtedly what stands out most is consistency, as KO gained global value participation for the 18th consecutive quarter. And, frankly, for me that regularity is worth more than one or two points of additional growth.

Cash Flow and Balance: Behind the Noise, the Same Solidity as Always

When I look back at the cash flow numbers, the first thing I do is separate the extraordinary from what really matters. And in this quarter, that difference is immediately noticeable. The FCF may give a lukewarm impression at first, but once I get the one-time impact of contingent payment by fairlife, my reading is totally different. Until September, the adjusted FCF reached $8.5B, driven by better operational performance and lower taxes, although with a somewhat more moderate contribution from working capital. For me, this metric is the one that best reflects the actual cash generation of the business, and I see it as very solid.

The year-end guidance points to an adjusted FCF of at least $9.8B, a level that I consider more than healthy and fully aligned with what one expects from KO in a normal year.

The balance reinforces this idea. Net debt over EBITDA remains close to 1.8x, below the target range of 2-2.5x, which in practice gives Coca-Cola ample room to continue investing, financing growth in strategic categories such as proteins and premium beverages, and sustaining repurchases and dividends without unnecessary stress.

In other words, even after an extraordinary outlay, the company continues to operate with a comfortable, stable, and predictable capital structure.

Valuation, Growth, and Profitability: The Gap Remains

When I move from operational results to valuation, the feeling KO leaves me with is practically the same as I had in my previous analysis. The business operates with admirable precision, but the market pays so dearly for that stability that it leaves little room for a positive surprise.

While the P/E FWD is at 23.83x, 54% above the sector, the EV/EBITDA FWD stands at 21.10x, more than twice the average of comparable companies. Even P/S trades at a premium of over 400% against the sector. When I see all these multiples together, what I interpret is that the market not only recognizes the quality of KO but overcompensates for it.

Growth, while very solid, does not quite explain that gap. Revenues rose 2.8% YoY, EBITDA grew 11.39% YoY, and diluted EPS increased 25.34%, although on a favorable comparative basis. The estimated growth of EPS at 3-5 years is around 6.56%, a stable and predictable pace, but far from justifying such high multiples.

Profitability is undoubtedly where KO again shows its structural advantage. A gross margin of 61.63%, an EBIT margin of 31.93%, and a net margin of 27.34% are levels that most consumer companies can only look at from afar. And the ROE of 45.12%, along with an ROA of 12.29%, confirms that the company creates value consistently, even in years with macro shocks or complex FX.

But even with that extraordinary profitability, I find it hard to see a strong upside from these multiples. For me, the conclusion is almost the same as in September, and it is that while KO is a company of undisputed quality, the price reflects it completely. The market is paying a premium for sleeping well, and although I understand that, at this level it also limits potential returns for those who enter today.

Risks

At this point, the risks facing Coca-Cola are not a surprise, but they remain important. The first is FX, and as we saw this quarter, a negative impact can wipe out much of earnings. In large markets such as Mexico, this front is even more sensitive.

The second risk is pricing. While I note that KO managed to manage elasticities with great precision, that doesn’t mean it’s automatic. Each adjustment requires calibration by channel, by package, and by economic context. And when the consumer begins to feel pressure, each price point demands more subtlety.

Another risk to consider is regulatory. Every day there are more sugar taxes, changes in packaging, and environmental standards that can generate additional costs or restrictions. While this is not new and Coca-Cola has been handling these issues for decades, it is a structural risk that never goes away.

Still, I point out that the company has two advantages that balance this risk map, and these are global scale and operational discipline. In my experience following KO, these two factors have allowed repeated shocks to be absorbed without affecting the long-term vision.

Conclusion

After reviewing Q3, my feeling about Coca-Cola didn’t change. I see a company that continues to run with precision, defending margins, gaining share, and showing a consistency that few global companies can replicate. Quarter by quarter, the data confirms that the business model works even when the environment does not follow.

But, at the same time, the other side of the story also becomes clear again, and it is that the stock trades at demanding multiples, still above its historical averages and with a marked premium against the sector. So, although KO provides predictability, stable flow, and profitability that stand out in any comparison, the current valuation leaves little room for further expansion.

For defensive and revenue-oriented profiles, KO remains almost a reference, but for those looking for more aggressive upside, the entry still seems to me tight.

For all this, I keep my position in hold. It is an excellent business, but today it is purchased at a price that fully reflects that excellence.

data.title: Oportun Financial: Trading At A Discount To Book Value Amid Macro Uncertainty
data.description:
Oportun Financial (OPRT) posted Q3 results earlier this month. While the stock appears to be down amid fears of the macro situation, the business is in a steady state, at a discount to its tangible book value, and so I've maintained my Buy rating.

Summary of Previous Thesis

When I covered OPRT last time, I noted that it was trading at a discount to tangible book value per share, despite being profitable. This seemed tied to concerns in the wider market about potential impacts of President Trump's tariffs, the then-lack of rate cuts by the Fed in 2025, and how these might impact the nonprime borrowers on Oportun's loan book.

As a reminder, Oportun gives personal loans to nonprime borrowers, which are typically unsecured by any collateral and rely on high APRs (up to 36%) to price in the underwriting risk.

Nevertheless, the book was showing quarterly improvements in net charge-offs and delinquencies. Not only that, but cost-cutting measures were also being successfully implemented. I felt the discount to TBV per share was a reasonable case for undervaluation and rated them a Buy.

Q3 Updates

Oportun delivered another profitable quarter, their fourth consecutive one.

While GAAP income was down slightly from Q2, it reflected a large improvement from the prior-year quarter. The profits aren't just good on their own. The balance sheet as of Q3 shows the improvements made in their capital structure.

This is largely seen in the shift of their secured and asset-backed financing. During the earnings call, management explained:

We executed ABS financings at weighted average yields below 6% in August and October and proactively repaid higher cost corporate debt. Additionally, we expanded our warehouse financing capacity in October by adding a new facility and modifying an existing one, extending average maturity and reducing our average cost of capital.

Above, I've indicated the portions of their ABS borrowings and the changes between the end of 2024 and the end of Q3. The lower-cost portions have been kept intact, while the higher-cost portions were rolled into new ABS issues. Management said of this:

...cost of debt was lower sequentially, decreasing from 8.6% in the second quarter to 8.1% in the third quarter, closely aligning with our 8% unit economics target.

As far as total leverage is concerned, Q3 marked a streak of continued decreases, now at 7.1x debt-to-equity.

While we often look at net interest income and the balance sheet when analyzing lenders, it's also important to note Oportun's improvement in their overhead. Q3's operating expenses were down over $9M (11%) from the prior year, contributing to a $43M reduction year-to-date.

With attention on the underwriting, 30+ day delinquencies did tick up in the quarter, but net charge-offs remained stable.

Overall, it was a quarter that showed stable earnings, a steady loan book, improved capital structure, and more cost-effective overhead.

Outlook and Valuation

Let's revisit my previous metric for valuation: TBV per share, which we'll calculate with fully diluted shares outstanding.

Taking into account potentially dilutive RSUs, there were 48.3M shares outstanding in Q3. With this, common equity of $383.9M in Q3 and capitalized software and other intangibles of $74.8M, TBV per share comes to $6.40. The recent stock price of $4.80 is an attractive discount of 25%, and as long as they remain profitable, TBV per share should grow.

Management offered insights on cautious preparation for a macro environment showing signs of stress, stating in the call:

So we think that right now in this economy, it's important to try to decrease loan size and really focus on making payments affordable. And that's because though we think that the consumer today continues to be very resilient, there are certainly pressure points, right?

They also spoke to increasing the priority of their secured loan product in order to make the overall book lower in risk. I take these as signs that management is bracing for some impact, even though the current book is a "post-tightening" vintage. They understand that things can become harder.

The target debt-to-equity multiple is 6x, so earnings will be retained to improve that or reduce an outstanding debt obligation. As they continue to do this, I expect they can also refinance other portions of their debt at lower costs of capital, increasing EPS and the likelihood that OPRT is at a true discount.

Because of these remarks, I think a risk-managed entry price is in order, and I will readjust an entry price of $5.00 or less for this stock going forward.

Conclusion

Oportun isn't showing signs of trouble, but management is clearly preparing for that possibility, and so the current discount isn't too surprising. Investors can manage the risks by being disciplined on the entry price and the sizing. Because the discount seems sufficient at this point, however, I rate the stock a Buy.

data.title: Columbia International Dividend Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market overview

Per the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index, international markets built on their positive returns from the first half of the year, returning 6.89% in the third quarter. Ten of the eleven sectors within the benchmark posted a positive return during the quarter.

The third quarter of 2025 was marked by resilient, but uneven global growth, as economies navigated the lingering effects of elevated tariffs, shifting trade alliances and monetary policy recalibrations. Trade dynamics were a central theme throughout the quarter. Although some bilateral trade agreements — particularly between the U.S. and key partners like Japan and the E.U. — helped reduce uncertainty, the broader landscape was still shaped by protectionist policies. The U.S. implemented new tariffs on strategic imports such as copper, pharmaceuticals and consumer electronics, which contributed to rising input costs and inflationary pressures globally. These measures also prompted retaliatory actions from trading partners, further complicating supply chains and dampening cross-border investment flows. Central banks around the world continued to recalibrate their policy approaches during the third quarter, responding to a delicate balance of slowing growth, persistent inflation and evolving political pressures. The U.S. Federal Reserve made a pivotal move in September, cutting its benchmark interest rate to a range of 4.00–4.25%. In Europe, the European Central Bank (ECB) maintained its aggressive easing trajectory, executing its eighth rate cut since mid-2024. The deposit rate now stands at 2.15%, as the ECB seeks to stimulate growth amid weak industrial output and subdued consumer confidence.

From a country perspective, China and Japan were the top two contributing countries to index performance. China benefited from targeted fiscal stimulus, accommodative monetary policy and strong performance in high-tech and consumer sectors which helped offset deflationary pressures and trade-related headwinds. Japan’s equity market rallied, buoyed by improving corporate governance, foreign investor inflows and a favorable trade agreement with the U.S.

Sector weights (%): fund vs. benchmark as of September 30, 2025

Quarterly portfolio recap

Both stock selection and sector allocation detracted from the fund’s relative returns compared to the benchmark for the quarter.

Positive contributions to the fund’s relative performance were led by financials, industrials and consumer staples sectors while the consumer discretionary, materials and communication services sectors detracted most significantly on a relative basis. In country terms, South Korea and Ireland led positive relative contributions while the United Kingdom and Switzerland detracted most from relative returns.

In the financials sector, Bank of Ireland (OTCPK:BKRIF)(OTCPK:BKRIY) and the Japanese financial services company, Orix (IX), both contributed positively to returns. Orix shares rose after the company reported strong quarterly results. These results and an announcement that the company is selling its stake in Indian renewables company, Greenko, both increased confidence in Orix’s ability to reach its return on equity (ROE) target. Bank of Ireland performed strongly on news of a favorable outcome in a Supreme Court judgement related to historic Motor Finance deals in the U.K.

Mitsubishi Electric (OTCPK:MIELF)(OTCPK:MIELY) was the largest contributor to returns in the industrials sector. The stock reacted positively to signs that they are beginning to execute on plans to improve their profitability, such as by exiting lower margin businesses and carrying out some workforce restructuring.

Lastly, in the consumer staples sector, Reckitt Benckiser (OTCPK:RBGPF) was a relative outperformer after they announced an agreement to sell their homecare business. This development made it possible for them to raise their sales growth guidance and sanction a buyback program.

Turning to the detractors, the largest negative in the consumer discretionary sector was the U.K. homebuilder Taylor Wimpey (OTCPK:TWODF)(OTCPK:TWODY), which saw earnings forecasts downgraded due to the weak U.K. housing backdrop.

The fund also suffered from not owning Alibaba (BABA), the Chinese e-commerce and cloud computing giant, which rose significantly over the quarter after the company’s results showed continued acceleration in their cloud business.

In the materials sector, the ingredients business DSM performed poorly on news that the planned sale of their animal health division was progressing more slowly than anticipated.

Lastly, in the communication services sector, Deutsche Telekom (OTCQX:DTEGY)(OTCQX:DLAKF) shares fell after weaker results from their German telecoms division raised concerns that competition in the industry may be increasing.

Outlook

The market remains swayed by ongoing uncertainty around tariffs, monetary policy and geopolitics along with the huge transition taking place in the world’s technology systems towards AI. On the one hand, the boom in data center capital expenditure (capex) and the associated growth in demand for power are driving strong growth in many areas of the semiconductor and industrial complexes. On the other hand, other key areas of economic activity remain challenged, such as the residential construction sector and lower income consumers. Against this backdrop, we maintain a balance in the portfolio between more cyclical companies that are positioned to benefit from those areas of the economy that are performing strongly and some more defensively positioned businesses.

Across all regions and sectors, we continue to look for high and growing free cash flow, sustainable profit margins, intelligent capital allocation and strong balance sheets. The focus on these quality factors has benefited the relative performance of the portfolio in times of stress and over full market cycles.
