data.title: Gamco Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust is an income fund. The Fund intends to generate current income from short-term gains primarily through its strategy of writing (selling) covered call options on the equity securities in its portfolio. Because of its primary strategy, the Fund forgoes the opportunity to participate fully in the appreciation of the underlying equity security above the exercise price of the option. It also is subject to the risk of depreciation of the underlying equity security in excess of the premium received.

Investment Objective

The GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust is a non-diversified, closed-end management investment company. The Fund’s investment objective is to provide a high level of current income. The Fund’s secondary investment objective is to seek capital appreciation consistent with the Fund’s strategy and primary objective. Under normal market conditions, the Fund will attempt to achieve its objectives by investing 80% of its assets in equity securities of companies principally engaged in natural resource and gold industries, and by writing covered call options on the underlying equity securities.

Investment Discussion

Gold's exceptional momentum continues after a spectacular first half of 2025 in which gold surged 25.9%, the third quarter bringing no respite for bearish investors. Instead of the anticipated consolidation, the precious metal rally gained speed, fueled by the escalating U.S. fiscal crisis and furthered powered by the passage of the Big Beautiful Bill. Gold bullion rose an additional 16.8% in the third quarter, driving its year-to-date increase to a staggering 47.1%. The factors driving this flight to gold and the parallel trend of de-dollarization are numerous and structural: high U.S. debt-to-GDP ratio, high interest rates on that debt, a large budget deficit, geopolitical volatility, tariff volatility, and the "weaponization" of the dollar. As the global financial system searches for an alternative, gold remains the "shining angel" in the absence of many credible fiat alternatives. A further reduction in interest rates could amplify its appeal even more. Investor interest was strong, with private investors adding over 6 million ounces to gold-backed exchange-traded funds (ETFs) during the quarter. Total gold holdings across all ETFs reached 96.7 million ounces at the end of September, up from 82.9 million ounces at the start of the year. However, this is still well below the 2020 high of 111 million ounces, suggesting the rally may have room to run. Mirroring the commodity's performance, the Philadelphia Gold and Silver Index (XAU), a key benchmark for mining stocks, returned 44.9% for the quarter, reflecting a fair valuation of the underlying gold price increase.

In contrast to gold, the oil sector continues to struggle with oversupply. Pricing for West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude fluctuated, driven primarily by geopolitical dynamics rather than underlying demand, ultimately dropping 4.2% for the quarter. Despite the price drop, the market saw refining margins continue their downward trend, averaging around $25 per barrel. U.S. production remained steady at 13.5 million barrels per day, and OPEC production saw an expected increase, hitting 29 million barrels per day. While the commodity itself underperformed, energy equities, as represented by the Energy Select Sector Index (IXE), expanded by 6.3%. This gain suggests the equity market is performing more in line with the general market and discounting some of the near-term commodity price weakness.

During the third quarter of 2025, the agricultural sector, represented by the MVIS Global Agribusiness Index, was flat, while the general market was up 8.1%. The performance was driven mostly by Elanco Corp. (ELAN) +41.0%, Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) +14.1%, Deere (DE) -9.8%, and Bayer (BAYN) +10.3%.

Volatility levels at the end of the third quarter increased in all sectors, with the gold sector at 38%, 32% for the base metals sector, 32% for agriculture, and 26% for energy equities. We have maintained a part of the gold equity section of the fund unhedged during the quarter. The maturity of the option portfolio was an average of 2.5 months. At the end of the quarter, the Fund’s participation across sectors was 55% for gold and mining, 48% for agriculture, and 50% for energy.

Premium/Discount Discussion

As a refresher for our shareholders, the price of a closed-end fund is determined in the open market by willing buyers and sellers. Closed-End Fund shares trade on an exchange and may trade at a premium to (higher than) net asset value (the market value of the Fund’s underlying portfolio and other assets less any liabilities) or a discount to (lower than) net asset value. Ideally, a Fund’s market price will generally track the NAV. However, a Fund’s premium or discount to NAV may vary over time.
